JUNIOR_DEBATE_FORMAT_AND_RULES
Document type: Judge training material
Format: WSDA Junior Extemporaneous Debate
Primary users: Junior debate judges
Purpose: Explain Junior Debate format, topic release, preparation procedure, speech order, timing, prep time, evidence use, side confirmation, and judge escalation rules.

SECTION 1: WHAT JUNIOR DEBATE IS

Junior Debate is an extemporaneous debate format.

Each round has one pro debater and one con debater.

nior Debate is an extemporaneous debate format.

Each round has one pro debater and one con debater.

The two debaters debate one resolution or topic.

The system randomly assigns each debater's side.

The pro debater supports the resolution.

The con debater opposes the resolution.

In every round, the pro debater always gives the first speech.

SECTION 2: TOPIC SELECTION

In each round and each flight, debaters will receive a different independent topic.

TOPIC SELECTION

In each round and each flight, debaters will receive a different independent topic.

Debaters must prepare the assigned topic within the required preparation time.

The debate topic for each round will usually be announced in the judge WeChat group; if the judges are unsure about the topic, they should ask in the group to obtain clarification

To protect fairness, the extemporaneous topic should be released only 15 minutes before the official start of that round.

the extemporaneous topic should be released only 15 minutes before the official start of that round.

SECTION 3: PREPARATION GROUPS

After an extemporaneous topic is released, both pro and con debaters have 15 minutes to prepare.

During preparation, debaters may not receive outside assistance.

After preparation groups are assigned, the side generally should not be changed.

The round cannot be changed once confirmed unless tournament staff or the tabroom instructs otherwise.

e round cannot be changed once confirmed unless tournament staff or the tabroom instructs otherwise.

SECTION 4: WHEN DEBATERS ENTER THE ROOM

There are two possible situations when debaters enter the competition room.

In the first situation, debaters have already completed their 15-minute preparation in a separate room or preparation area. If this is the case, the judge should confirm the debaters, confirm their sides, and start the debate right away.

e case, the judge should confirm the debaters, confirm their sides, and start the debate right away.

In the second situation, debaters have not yet received the topic or are not aware of the topic. If this is the case, the judge should not start the debate immediately.

The judge should first confirm that both debaters are in the correct room.

The judge should confirm which debater is pro and which debater is con.

The judge should release the debate topic exactly as instructed in the WeChat group chat or official tournament communication.

The judge should make sure both debaters understand the topic and know which side they are debating before preparation begins.

The judge should start a 15-minute timer for preparation.

When the 15-minute preparation time is over, the judge should start the round.

SECTION 5: SIDE CONFIRMATION

Before every Junior Debate round, the judge should confirm each debater's side.

This side confirmation applies whether the debaters prepared in another room or will prepare inside the competition room.

may affect fairness and tournament results.

SECTION 6: UNEXPECTED ISSUES BEFORE OR DURING THE ROUND

If an unexpected issue happens, the judge should report it to the tabroom and wait for further instruction.

7. there is disagreement about whether preparation time has already occurred.

Judges should not independently change the round, switch debater sides, restart preparation, or make a penalty decision unless instructed by the tabroom or tournament staff.

SECTION 7: OVERTIME RULE

Debaters must stop speaking immediately when their speech time ends.

Debaters may not continue speaking after time expires.

Judges should apply the time rule consistently to both sides.

continue speaking after time expires.

Judges should apply the time rule consistently to both sides.

Arguments made after time expires should normally not receive weight.

SECTION 8: MATERIAL AND INTERNET USE

Online competitions allow debaters to use computers and internet search to find materials.

However, debaters may not receive outside assistance in any form.

nternet search to find materials.

However, debaters may not receive outside assistance in any form.

Outside assistance includes help from coaches, teachers, parents, other students, or anyone outside the round or preparation group.

If outside assistance is discovered, the competitor may be disqualified according to tournament rules. If the judge suspects such action, they should consult the tabroom for further assistance.

nt rules. If the judge suspects such action, they should consult the tabroom for further assistance.

In offline competitions, during preparation after the topic is released and during the debate itself, debaters may not use paper materials, electronic devices, the internet, or external evidence lookup.

Because preparation time is limited, the use of evidence is not the key deciding factor in Junior Debate.

se preparation time is limited, the use of evidence is not the key deciding factor in Junior Debate.

Evidence may be used if the format and tournament setting allow it, but judges should not treat evidence as the main evaluation factor.

Judges should focus more on analysis, reasoning, rebuttal, cross examination, delivery, and overall impact.

SECTION 9: ROUND LENGTH AND MAIN STAGES

Each Junior Debate round usually lasts about 15 to 20 minutes.

TION 9: ROUND LENGTH AND MAIN STAGES

Each Junior Debate round usually lasts about 15 to 20 minutes.

The round has four main stages:
1. constructive speeches;
2. cross examination;
3. rebuttal speeches;
4. final focus or summary speeches.

Each speech is usually 1 to 2 minutes long, depending on the specific stage.

Judges should follow the official timing table for the round.

SECTION 10: Procedure for junior debate

The round follows this order:

SECTION 10: Procedure for junior debate

The round follows this order:

1. Pro Constructive: 2 minutes.
2. Cross Examination of the Pro by the Con: 1 minute.
3. Con Constructive: 2 minutes.
4. Cross Examination of the Con by the Pro: 1 minute.
5. Mandatory Prep Time: 1 minute.
6. Pro Rebuttal: 2 minutes.
7. Con Rebuttal: 2 minutes.
8. Mandatory Prep Time: 1 minute.
9. Pro Final Focus: 2 minutes.
10. Con Final Focus: 2 minutes.

The con debater asks questions after the pro constructive.

Both debaters receive mandatory preparation time before rebuttals and before final focus speeches.

SECTION 11: CONSTRUCTIVE SPEECHES AND CROSS EXAMINATION

SECTION 11: CONSTRUCTIVE SPEECHES AND CROSS EXAMINATION

In the constructive speech, each debater presents their position.

The pro constructive presents a case or position in favor of the topic.

The con constructive presents a case or position against the topic.

After each constructive speech, the opposing debater asks questions.

During cross examination, the questioner should ask questions and the speaker being questioned should answer.

oss examination, the questioner should ask questions and the speaker being questioned should answer.

A debater should keep the correct role during cross examination.

The debater being questioned should not avoid answering, refuse to answer, or turn the cross examination into their own questioning period.

If a debater fails to keep the correct role during cross examination, the debater may lose points.

If a debater fails to keep the correct role during cross examination, the debater may lose points.

In extreme cases, serious refusal to participate properly in cross examination may cause the judge to vote against that side.

SECTION 12: REBUTTAL SPEECHES

In rebuttal speeches, debaters should respond to the opponent's main ideas.

A debater should identify the key problems in the opponent's arguments or reasoning.

A debater may show that the opponent's theory is flawed.

in the opponent's arguments or reasoning.

A debater may show that the opponent's theory is flawed.

A debater may turn the opponent's reasoning against them.

A debater may present effective responses against the opponent's arguments.

A rebuttal should not only repeat the debater's own constructive speech.

SECTION 13: FINAL FOCUS OR SUMMARY SPEECHES

In final focus or summary speeches, debaters summarize the main points of disagreement.

Each final focus speech is 2 minutes.

speeches, debaters summarize the main points of disagreement.

Each final focus speech is 2 minutes.

Debaters should explain why they should win the debate.

Debaters should not introduce new arguments in this final stage.

The final speech should focus on the existing clash between the two sides.

SECTION 14: MANDATORY PREP TIME

There is 1 minute of mandatory prep time after the constructive and cross examination stage.

P TIME

There is 1 minute of mandatory prep time after the constructive and cross examination stage.

There is another 1 minute of mandatory prep time after the rebuttal stage.

This prep time must be used.

This prep time may not be divided into smaller parts.

Judges should time the mandatory prep periods and start the next speech when prep time is over.

SECTION 15: QUICK REFERENCE FOR JUDGES

Junior Debate is one pro debater versus one con debater.

Sides are randomly assigned by the system.

Junior Debate is one pro debater versus one con debater.

If debaters already prepared in a separate room, confirm sides and start the debate.

If debaters have not received the topic, release the topic as instructed, confirm sides, start 15 minutes of preparation, and then begin the round.

The round cannot be changed once confirmed unless tournament staff instructs otherwise.

JUNIOR_DEBATE_SCORING
Document type: Judge training material
Format: WSDA Junior Extemporaneous Debate
Primary users: Junior debate judges
Purpose: Explain Junior Debate judging criteria, score range, point calculation, ranking, and ballot expectations.

SECTION 1: SCORING OVERVIEW

Junior Debate is scored from 18 to 30 points.

18 points is the base score.

Judges should add points above 18 based on the debater's performance in the six evaluation criteria.

Judges should add points above 18 based on the debater's performance in the six evaluation criteria.

The six evaluation criteria are:
1. Analysis.
2. Reasoning.
3. Rebuttal.
4. Cross Examination.
5. Delivery.
6. Overall Impact.

Judges should score based on what the debater does in the round.

SECTION 2: HOW TO ADD POINTS FROM 18

Judges should begin from the base score of 18.

For each of the six evaluation criteria, the judge may add points according to performance.

of 18.

For each of the six evaluation criteria, the judge may add points according to performance.

If the debater's performance in a criterion is insufficient, add 0 points for that criterion.

If the debater reaches an average level in a criterion, add 0.5 points for that criterion.

If the debater is above average in a criterion, add 1 point for that criterion.

If the debater is strong in a criterion, add 1.5 points for that criterion.

int for that criterion.

If the debater is strong in a criterion, add 1.5 points for that criterion.

If the debater is very strong and clearly stands out in a criterion, add 2 points for that criterion.

Because there are six criteria and each criterion can add up to 2 points, the maximum score is 30.

The scoring formula is:
18 base points plus added points from the six criteria equals the final score.

SECTION 3: CRITERION 1 - ANALYSIS

s plus added points from the six criteria equals the final score.

SECTION 3: CRITERION 1 - ANALYSIS

Analysis evaluates whether the student explains the main issue of the topic.

A strong debater should look at the issue from an objective angle.

A strong debater should present arguments that have some impact.

A strong debater should clearly explain their own arguments.

Weak analysis may rely only on personal preference or unsupported opinion.

ain their own arguments.

Weak analysis may rely only on personal preference or unsupported opinion.

For example, saying "I think cats are better than dogs because my father said so" is not persuasive analysis.

Judges should ask:
1. Did the debater identify the main issue?
2. Did the debater explain the issue clearly?
3. Did the argument have impact?
4. Did the debater avoid relying only on personal opinion?

SECTION 4: CRITERION 2 - REASONING

pact?
4. Did the debater avoid relying only on personal opinion?

SECTION 4: CRITERION 2 - REASONING

Reasoning evaluates the student's logical support.

Judges should consider whether the debater's explanation is logical.

Judges should consider whether the speech connects ideas clearly.

Judges should consider whether claims are supported by reasons.

Strong reasoning makes it clear why the judge should believe the argument.

re supported by reasons.

Strong reasoning makes it clear why the judge should believe the argument.

Weak reasoning may jump from a claim to a conclusion without enough explanation.

SECTION 5: CRITERION 3 - REBUTTAL

Rebuttal evaluates whether the student effectively responds to the opponent's arguments.

A strong rebuttal shows the judge why the opponent's argument is flawed.

A debater may attack the opponent's assumption, logic, example, impact, or conclusion.

t is flawed.

A debater may attack the opponent's assumption, logic, example, impact, or conclusion.

A rebuttal should be specific.

A weak rebuttal may ignore the opponent's main argument or only repeat the debater's own case.

Judges should ask:
1. Did the debater answer the opponent's main point?
2. Was the response specific?
3. Did the response explain why the opponent's argument is weak?
4. Did the debater avoid missing important arguments?

SECTION 6: CRITERION 4 - CROSS EXAMINATION

k?
4. Did the debater avoid missing important arguments?

SECTION 6: CRITERION 4 - CROSS EXAMINATION

Cross Examination evaluates the interaction between the two debaters during questioning.

Judges should consider whether the debater asks and answers clearly, concisely, and politely.

A strong question should help clarify the debate or challenge the opponent's reasoning.

A strong answer should respond to the question and defend the debater's position.

nent's reasoning.

A strong answer should respond to the question and defend the debater's position.

A debater should actively explain their own reasoning and effectively challenge the opponent's argument.

A weak cross examination may be rude, unclear, evasive, too long, or unrelated to the main issues.

Judges should also consider whether the debater keeps the correct role as questioner or answerer.

SECTION 7: CRITERION 5 - DELIVERY

Delivery evaluates the debater's speaking style.

er or answerer.

SECTION 7: CRITERION 5 - DELIVERY

Delivery evaluates the debater's speaking style.

Judges should consider whether the debater's expression is clear, accurate, efficient, and not unnecessarily slow or repetitive.

Strong delivery helps the judge understand the argument.

Weak delivery may make the debate hard to follow even if the idea itself is reasonable.

Delivery includes clarity, organization, pace, volume, fluency, confidence, and time control.

able.

Delivery includes clarity, organization, pace, volume, fluency, confidence, and time control.

Judges should not reward style alone if the argument has weak analysis or reasoning.

SECTION 8: CRITERION 6 - OVERALL IMPACT

Overall Impact evaluates the persuasive effect of the whole debate performance.

Judges should consider whether the debater's performance is convincing and influential overall.

ce.

Judges should consider whether the debater's performance is convincing and influential overall.

A strong overall impact means the debater gives the judge a clear reason to prefer their side.

Overall impact may come from strong analysis, clear reasoning, effective rebuttal, good cross examination, and persuasive delivery working together.

A weak overall impact means the performance does not leave a clear reason to vote for that side.

SECTION 9: EVIDENCE AND SCORING

he performance does not leave a clear reason to vote for that side.

SECTION 9: EVIDENCE AND SCORING

Use of evidence is allowed when the tournament setting permits it.

However, evidence is not the key evaluating factor for Junior Debate.

Because Junior Debate uses limited preparation time, judges should not decide the round mainly based on who has more evidence.

Judges should focus more on explanation, logic, interaction, rebuttal, delivery, and overall persuasiveness.

hould focus more on explanation, logic, interaction, rebuttal, delivery, and overall persuasiveness.

A debater who uses evidence should still explain what the evidence proves and why it matters.

A debater who does not use evidence can still score well if their analysis, reasoning, rebuttal, cross examination, delivery, and overall impact are strong.

SECTION 10: RANKING AND DECISION

Judges should decide which debater performed better in the round.

SECTION 10: RANKING AND DECISION

Judges should decide which debater performed better in the round.

Judges should use the six evaluation criteria to guide both scoring and ranking.

The higher-scoring debater should normally be ranked ahead.

Judges should avoid score-rank inconsistency (no lower point wins).

Judges should not decide based only on speaking confidence, personal agreement with the topic, or evidence quantity.

t decide based only on speaking confidence, personal agreement with the topic, or evidence quantity.

Judges should not introduce their own arguments or outside knowledge into the decision.

SECTION 11: BALLOT EXPECTATIONS

The Junior Debate ballot should include the topic, competitor names, competitor numbers, score, rank, and comments according to the tournament system.

Judges should record competitor information carefully.

Judges should make sure the score reflects the six criteria.

cord competitor information carefully.

Judges should make sure the score reflects the six criteria.

Judges should make sure the final score is calculated from 18 plus added points for the criteria.

Comments should explain the main strengths and weaknesses of the debater's performance.

Useful comments may mention analysis, reasoning, rebuttal, cross examination, delivery, or overall impact.

Junior Debate scores range from 18 to 30.

JUNIOR_SPEECH_COMPETITION_RULES
Document type: Judge training material
Format: WSDA Junior Speech
Primary users: Junior speech judges
Purpose: Explain the main competition rules for Junior Speech, including time limit, originality, cue cards, and props.

SECTION 1: DOCUMENT PURPOSE

This document explains the basic rules judges should know when evaluating Junior Speech.

Judges should use these rules to understand what is allowed during the speech and what should or should not affect scoring.

rules to understand what is allowed during the speech and what should or should not affect scoring.

SECTION 2: SPEECH TIME LIMIT

The time limit for Junior Speech is 3 minutes.

A speaker may give a speech shorter than 3 minutes.

A speech shorter than 3 minutes should not be penalized only because it is shorter.

When the speech reaches 3 minutes, the speaker must stop immediately.

Judges should not require a minimum speech length.

es 3 minutes, the speaker must stop immediately.

Judges should not require a minimum speech length.

Judges should evaluate whether the speech is effective within the time used.

SECTION 3: ORIGINALITY RULE

The speech does not have to be original.

A speaker may write an original speech.

A speaker may also revise, add to, excerpt from, or directly use an existing speech written by someone else.

Judges should not use originality as a scoring standard in Junior Speech.

written by someone else.

Judges should not use originality as a scoring standard in Junior Speech.

Judges should not penalize a speaker simply because the speech content is adapted from or based on another existing text.

SECTION 4: CUE CARD RULE

Speakers are encouraged to deliver the speech from memory.

However, speakers are allowed to use cue cards during the speech.

Whether the speaker is fully memorized should not be treated as an independent scoring standard.

h.

Whether the speaker is fully memorized should not be treated as an independent scoring standard.

A speaker should not automatically receive a lower score only because they used cue cards.

However, judges may consider the actual effect of cue card use on performance.

Cue cards may reduce eye contact with the judge and audience.

Cue cards may affect fluency, confidence, audience connection, and the final presentation effect.

.

Cue cards may affect fluency, confidence, audience connection, and the final presentation effect.

Judges should evaluate how the speech is delivered, not simply whether cue cards were used.

SECTION 5: PROP RULE

Speakers may not use self-provided props during the speech, except for cue cards.

Speakers may not use PowerPoint slides.

Speakers may not use assistive props.

Speakers may not use other presentation tools that support or replace the speech.

assistive props.

Speakers may not use other presentation tools that support or replace the speech.

Junior Speech should be evaluated based on the speaker's speech content and speech delivery.

If a speaker uses a prohibited prop or tool, the judge should follow tournament procedure or report the issue to tournament staff.

SECTION 6: WHAT JUDGES SHOULD REMEMBER

The speech time limit is 3 minutes.

There is no minimum speech time.

Primary users: Junior speech judges
Purpose: Explain Junior Speech scoring, ranking, feedback levels, and the ten judging criteria.

SECTION 1: SCORING AND RANKING OVERVIEW

Judges evaluate each speaker based on the speaker's overall performance.

Judges give each speaker a score and a rank within the group.

The score range for Junior Speech is 90 to 100 points.

The score range for Junior Speech is 90 to 100 points.

A higher score should correspond to a higher ranking in the group.

Judges should rank speakers according to their relative performance in the same group.

SECTION 2: FEEDBACK LEVELS

The ballot includes ten judging criteria.

For each criterion, judges may mark a feedback level.

The five feedback levels are:
1. Below Average.
2. Average.
3. Above Average.
4. Good.
5. Excellent.

1. Below Average.

The feedback levels do not directly determine the final score or rank.

A judge should use the feedback levels to help the speaker understand strengths and weaknesses.

The final score and rank should still be based on the speaker's overall performance.

SECTION 3: CRITERION 1 - ARTICULATION

Articulation evaluates the speaker's English pronunciation.

SECTION 3: CRITERION 1 - ARTICULATION

Articulation evaluates the speaker's English pronunciation.

Judges should consider whether the speaker's pronunciation is clear and understandable.

Judges should consider whether the speaker uses correct pronunciation patterns.

Strong articulation helps the judge and audience understand the speech.

Weak articulation may make the speech difficult to follow.

SECTION 4: CRITERION 2 - EMPHASIS

Emphasis evaluates whether the speaker highlights important words, phrases, and sentences.

A speaker may use pauses, stronger volume, or vocal stress to show emphasis.

Good emphasis helps the audience identify important ideas.

Weak emphasis may make the speech sound flat or make key points difficult to notice.

SECTION 5: CRITERION 3 - SPEAKING RATE

Speaking rate evaluates whether the speaker's speed is appropriate.

A speaker should not speak so quickly that the judge cannot understand the main content.

A speaker should not speak so slowly that the speech has long pauses or loses energy.

A strong speaking rate is controlled, understandable, and appropriate for the speech content.

controlled, understandable, and appropriate for the speech content.

SECTION 6: CRITERION 4 - VOLUME

Volume evaluates whether the speaker controls voice level appropriately.

The speaker should be loud enough for the judge and audience to hear clearly.

The speaker should avoid speaking too softly.

The speaker should also avoid shouting or using volume in a way that feels uncontrolled.

Good volume supports clarity and audience engagement.

SECTION 7: CRITERION 5 - FACIAL EXPRESSION

Facial expression evaluates whether the speaker uses facial expression effectively.

This includes eye contact with the judge and audience.

The speaker should not look only at cue cards or the floor.

Good facial expression helps create connection with the audience.

Weak facial expression may make the speech feel less engaging.

SECTION 8: CRITERION 6 - HAND GESTURES

Hand gestures evaluate whether the speaker uses appropriate gestures and movements during the speech.

Gestures should support the meaning of the speech.

Gestures should not feel stiff, distracting, random, or overly artificial.

Speakers should avoid playing with clothing or making distracting repeated movements.

SECTION 9: CRITERION 7 - BODY POSITION

Body position evaluates posture and stage position.

The speaker should show appropriate posture.

The speaker should avoid standing completely still in a way that weakens the presentation.

The speaker should also avoid walking around constantly in a distracting way.

Good body position supports confidence, control, and stage presence.

nstantly in a distracting way.

SECTION 10: CRITERION 8 - CREATIVITY OF CONTENT

Creativity of content evaluates whether the speech content is interesting, fresh, or imaginative.

The content does not have to be original.

A speaker may quote or use creative content from others or outside sources.

The judging focus is not whether the idea was originally created by the speaker.

The judging focus is how the speaker uses creative ideas in the speech.

SECTION 11: CRITERION 9 - ORGANIZATION OF CONTENT

Organization of content evaluates whether the speech is logical and well structured.

The parts of the speech should connect to each other.

er the speech is logical and well structured.

SECTION 12: CRITERION 10 - OVERALL IMPACT

Overall impact evaluates the total effect of the speech.

SECTION 12: CRITERION 10 - OVERALL IMPACT

Overall impact evaluates the total effect of the speech.

Judges should consider whether the speech is effective as a whole.

Overall impact includes content, delivery, clarity, organization, creativity, and audience connection.

A speech with strong overall impact leaves a clear impression on the judge and audience.

A speech with weak overall impact may be understandable but not especially effective.

In Original Oratory, the junior division speech time limit is 5 minutes.

The senior division speech time limit is 10 minutes.

All divisions have an additional 30-second grace period.

Public Forum debate, often called PF, is a two-person team debate format. Each side has two debaters. One team supports the resolution, and the other team opposes the resolution.

SECTION 14: COMMON VOTING MISTAKES TO AVOID

Do not vote based only on speaking confidence.

Do not vote based only on which side had more evidence.

Do not ignore the final speeches.

SECTION 15: BALLOT EXPLANATION

After deciding the winner, the judge should write a reason for decision.

The reason for decision should explain why one team won over the other. It should identify the main voting issue or issues and connect them to the arguments in the round.
